request.freeText: my online shopping is out of control

response.scoreModifierReason: Near-zero liquid savings with an acknowledged impulse spending habit creates a fragile buffer that the CFPB scale doesn't fully penalize on its own.
response.summary: You're a full-time earner in a medium cost-of-living state with almost nothing saved and an online shopping habit you already know is a problem. That combo — tiny cushion, leaky spending — means one bad month turns into debt fast. The income is workable; the behavior is the variable.
response.roast: You've got less than a car payment in savings and your cart is basically a second rent. The "add to cart" button is doing more work than your 401k right now.
insights: Near-zero savings means any unexpected expense — car repair, medical bill — goes straight to a credit card.
Online shopping leaks are dangerous precisely because they feel small per transaction but stack into hundreds monthly.
In a medium COL state like TX, your income bracket should leave room to save — the gap is behavioral, not structural.
Without a named budget for discretionary spending, 'out of control' stays out of control — visibility is the first fix.
Building even a $1,000 emergency buffer would break the debt spiral before it starts.
topProblems: Virtually no liquid savings — one surprise expense away from debt.
Unquantified but self-acknowledged online shopping habit draining discretionary budget.
No financial cushion to absorb income disruption or irregular expenses.
positiveBehaviors: You named the problem yourself — that's not nothing, most people don't.
Full-time employment in a medium COL state gives you real structural room to fix this.

topFix.action: Pull your last 60 days of bank/card statements, total every online purchase, then set a hard monthly cap and move that freed cash to a separate savings account on payday.
